AI adoption also affects the current account through imports of digital services, especially from the United States, as the deployment of AI systems also requires software, cloud computing capacity, data processing, model access and intellectual property.
Corporate gross saving fell from 12.3% of GDP in 2024 to 11.7% in 2025, while corporate gross capital formation rose from 12.0% to 12.3%.
Some AI-related spending by euro area firms is recorded as domestic investment, thereby raising gross capital formation and reducing net lending.

Surpluses were recorded for goods (€20 billion) and services (€14 billion).

current account balance in 2025","content":"# The narrowing of the euro area current account balance in 2025. **The euro area current account surplus narrowed markedly in 2025 under the impact of US trade tariffs, the activities of US multinational enterprises (MNEs), structural shifts in global trade in goods, and rising digital and artificial intelligence (AI)-related investment.** The surplus declined to 1.7% of GDP in 2025, from 2.7% in 2024 (Chart A). **The narrowing in the euro area current account surplus in 2025 was mainly due to developments vis-à-vis the United States and China (Chart B).** The bilateral current account balance with the United States changed markedly, from a surplus of 0.1% of GDP in 2024 to a deficit of 0.4% in 2025, despite a larger goods surplus,
